pests.name: Aphids
pests.description: Small, soft-bodied insects that cluster on new growth and undersides of leaves. They suck plant sap and excrete honeydew, which attracts sooty mold.
symptoms: Curling, yellowing, or distorted leaves
Sticky honeydew residue on leaves and stems
Black sooty mold growing on honeydew
Stunted plant growth and wilting
Clusters of small green, black, or white insects on new growth
Ants farming aphids on plant surfaces
precautions: Inspect new plants before introducing them to your garden
Encourage natural predators like ladybugs, lacewings, and parasitic wasps
Avoid over-fertilizing with nitrogen, which promotes soft growth aphids love
Use reflective mulch to disorient flying aphids
Plant companion plants like marigolds, chives, and garlic nearby
Maintain proper plant spacing for good air circulation
remedies: Blast aphids off with a strong stream of water from a hose
Apply insecticidal soap spray directly on aphid clusters
Use neem oil spray (2 tbsp per gallon of water) every 7-14 days
Release ladybugs or lacewing larvae as biological control
Apply diatomaceous earth around the base of plants
For severe infestations, use pyrethrin-based organic insecticide
Remove heavily infested plant parts and dispose of them

pests.name: Whiteflies
pests.description: Tiny white-winged insects that congregate on leaf undersides. They feed on plant sap and can transmit viral diseases between plants.
symptoms: Cloud of tiny white insects flying when plant is disturbed
Yellowing leaves that eventually drop
Sticky honeydew on leaf surfaces
White waxy residue on leaf undersides
Stunted and weakened plant growth
Sooty mold development on honeydew deposits
precautions: Use yellow sticky traps to monitor and reduce populations
Inspect transplants carefully before planting
Remove and destroy crop residues after harvest
Use reflective aluminum mulch to repel whiteflies
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization
Install fine mesh screening on greenhouse vents
remedies: Apply insecticidal soap spray targeting leaf undersides
Use neem oil as a foliar spray every 5-7 days
Introduce Encarsia formosa parasitic wasps for biological control
Vacuum adult whiteflies with a handheld vacuum in early morning
Apply horticultural oil to smother eggs and nymphs
Use systemic insecticides for severe greenhouse infestations
Alternate between different modes of action to prevent resistance

pests.name: Spider Mites
pests.description: Microscopic arachnids that pierce plant cells and suck out contents. They thrive in hot, dry conditions and can devastate plants rapidly.
symptoms: Fine stippling or speckling on leaf surfaces (tiny yellow/white dots)
Fine silky webbing on leaf undersides and between stems
Leaves turning bronze, yellow, or brown
Leaf drop and defoliation in severe cases
Dried and crispy leaf edges
Reduced fruit size and quality
precautions: Keep plants well-watered; mites thrive in dry, stressed conditions
Mist plants regularly to increase humidity
Avoid broad-spectrum pesticides that kill mite predators
Quarantine new plants for 2 weeks before adding to collection
Keep garden area free of dust and debris
Encourage predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis)
remedies: Spray plants with a strong jet of water to dislodge mites
Apply miticide or acaricide specifically designed for spider mites
Use neem oil spray every 3-5 days for 2-3 weeks
Release predatory mites for long-term biological control
Apply insecticidal soap thoroughly, covering all leaf surfaces
Prune and destroy heavily infested branches
Use sulfur-based sprays (avoid on sulfur-sensitive plants)

pests.name: Caterpillars
pests.description: Larvae of butterflies and moths that chew through leaves, stems, and fruits. Some species like armyworms and hornworms can cause severe defoliation overnight.
symptoms: Large irregular holes chewed in leaves
Skeletonized leaves with only veins remaining
Dark green or black droppings (frass) on leaves
Rolled or webbed leaves sheltering larvae
Chewed fruit surfaces with bore holes
Defoliated stems and branches
precautions: Use floating row covers to prevent moth egg-laying
Encourage birds, parasitic wasps, and other natural predators
Hand-pick caterpillars during regular garden inspections
Plant trap crops to divert caterpillars from main crops
Use pheromone traps to monitor adult moth populations
Practice crop rotation to break pest cycles
remedies: Apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) spray — organic and highly effective
Hand-pick caterpillars and drop them in soapy water
Use spinosad-based organic insecticide
Apply neem oil to deter feeding and disrupt growth
Release Trichogramma wasps to parasitize caterpillar eggs
Use diatomaceous earth around plant bases
For severe infestations, apply carbaryl or permethrin (non-organic)

pests.name: Mealybugs
pests.description: Soft-bodied, white, cottony insects that feed on plant sap. They hide in leaf axils and crevices, making them difficult to detect and treat.
symptoms: White, cottony masses on stems, leaf joints, and undersides
Sticky honeydew on plant surfaces
Yellowing and wilting of affected leaves
Sooty mold growing on honeydew
Stunted growth and leaf drop
Deformed new growth and flowers
precautions: Inspect new plants thoroughly before purchase
Quarantine new plants for 2-3 weeks
Avoid over-watering and over-fertilizing
Keep plants clean and remove dead foliage
Improve air circulation around plants
Monitor plants regularly, especially in warm months
remedies: Dab individual mealybugs with rubbing alcohol using a cotton swab
Apply insecticidal soap spray, ensuring coverage in crevices
Use neem oil spray every 7-10 days
Release Cryptolaemus montrouzieri (mealybug destroyer ladybugs)
Apply systemic insecticide for severe root mealybug infestations
Prune heavily infested stems and dispose properly
Use horticultural oil to smother eggs and crawlers

pests.name: Thrips
pests.description: Tiny, slender insects that rasp plant tissue and feed on cell contents. They can transmit devastating plant viruses like Tomato Spotted Wilt Virus.
symptoms: Silvery or bronze streaks on leaves
Distorted, curled, or scarred leaves and petals
Black specks of excrement on leaf surfaces
Stunted growth and flower bud damage
Pitted or scarred fruit surfaces
Viral disease symptoms (ring spots, wilting)
precautions: Use blue or yellow sticky traps for monitoring
Remove weeds that serve as thrips breeding grounds
Mulch around plants to reduce thrips pupation in soil
Avoid planting susceptible crops near onion fields
Spray plants with water regularly to dislodge thrips
Encourage predatory insects like minute pirate bugs
remedies: Apply spinosad-based insecticide (very effective against thrips)
Use insecticidal soap with thorough coverage
Apply neem oil spray every 5-7 days
Release predatory mites (Amblyseius cucumeris) or minute pirate bugs
Use systemic insecticides for persistent infestations
Remove and destroy heavily infested plant material
Apply diatomaceous earth to soil surface to kill pupae

pests.name: Scale Insects
pests.description: Immobile, armored or soft-bodied insects that attach to stems and leaves. They blend in with bark and are often mistaken for natural bumps on the plant.
symptoms: Small, brown or tan bumps on stems and leaf undersides
Sticky honeydew coating on leaves (soft scale)
Yellowing and premature leaf drop
Branch dieback in severe infestations
Sooty mold on honeydew deposits
Reduced vigor and poor fruit production
precautions: Inspect nursery stock before purchase
Prune and destroy infested branches promptly
Encourage natural enemies like parasitic wasps and lady beetles
Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilizer
Maintain plant health through proper watering and nutrition
Monitor plants in spring when crawlers are active
remedies: Apply horticultural oil during dormant season (for deciduous plants)
Scrape off scale with a soft brush or fingernail
Apply insecticidal soap when crawlers are active
Use systemic insecticide (imidacloprid) for severe cases
Release parasitic wasps (Aphytis species) for biological control
Apply neem oil spray to suffocate scale insects
Prune and destroy heavily infested branches

pests.name: Leaf Miners
pests.description: Larvae of various flies, moths, and sawflies that tunnel between leaf surfaces, creating distinctive winding trails or blotches visible on the leaf.
symptoms: Serpentine (winding) white or light-colored trails in leaves
Blotch-shaped mines in leaf tissue
Leaves becoming papery thin in mined areas
Premature leaf drop in severe cases
Small puncture marks from adult feeding
Reduced photosynthesis and plant vigor
precautions: Use floating row covers to prevent adult flies from laying eggs
Remove and destroy affected leaves promptly
Encourage parasitic wasps that attack leaf miner larvae
Avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that kill natural enemies
Practice crop rotation
Clear garden debris at end of season
remedies: Remove and destroy mined leaves to eliminate larvae
Apply spinosad spray when adults are first seen
Use neem oil as a deterrent spray on new growth
Apply systemic insecticide for ornamental plants
Squish larvae inside the mines with your fingers
Release Diglyphus isaea parasitic wasps
Use sticky traps to monitor and capture adult miners

pests.name: Japanese Beetles
pests.description: Metallic green and copper beetles that skeletonize leaves and damage flowers and fruit. Their grubs also damage lawns by feeding on grass roots.
symptoms: Skeletonized leaves (veins intact, tissue eaten away)
Chewed flower petals and buds
Brown patches in lawns from grub root damage
Metallic green-copper beetles visible on plants
Clusters of beetles feeding in groups
Damaged fruit with feeding scars
precautions: Apply milky spore disease to lawns to control grubs long-term
Use beneficial nematodes (Heterorhabditis) in soil for grub control
Avoid Japanese beetle traps near gardens (they attract more beetles)
Plant resistant varieties when possible
Hand-pick beetles in early morning when they are sluggish
Maintain healthy lawns to withstand grub damage
remedies: Hand-pick beetles and drop into soapy water
Apply neem oil spray to reduce feeding and egg-laying
Use carbaryl (Sevin) spray for severe infestations
Apply milky spore powder to lawns to kill grubs over 2-3 years
Release beneficial nematodes in late summer for grub control
Spray with pyrethrin-based insecticide for quick knockdown
Use row covers to protect high-value crops during beetle season

pests.name: Fungus Gnats
pests.description: Small, dark flies whose larvae feed on organic matter and plant roots in moist soil. Primarily a problem with houseplants and greenhouse plants.
symptoms: Small dark flies hovering around soil surface
Wilting or yellowing of seedlings and young plants
Slow growth despite adequate care
Root damage visible when plant is uprooted
Larvae visible as tiny white worms in moist soil
Loss of seedlings (damping off)
precautions: Allow soil surface to dry between waterings
Use well-draining potting mix with perlite
Avoid organic mulch on indoor plant soil surfaces
Bottom-water plants when possible
Use yellow sticky traps near plants to monitor populations
Cover soil surface with sand or decorative stones
remedies: Let the top 1-2 inches of soil dry completely between waterings
Apply Bacillus thuringiensis israelensis (BTI) to soil (Mosquito Bits)
Use yellow sticky traps to capture adult gnats
Apply beneficial nematodes (Steinernema feltiae) to soil
Top-dress soil with 1/2 inch of sand to prevent egg-laying
Use hydrogen peroxide solution (1:4 with water) as a soil drench
Apply neem oil soil drench to kill larvae

pests.name: Tomato Hornworm
pests.description: Large green caterpillars (up to 4 inches) with distinctive horn on their rear. They can rapidly defoliate tomato and related plants, consuming entire leaves overnight.
symptoms: Rapid defoliation — entire stems stripped of leaves
Large dark green droppings (frass) on leaves and ground
Chewed or missing fruit with large bore holes
Bare stems where leaves have been completely consumed
Large (3-4 inch) green caterpillars with white V-marks on sides
Wilting branch tips from stem damage
precautions: Inspect tomato plants daily during growing season
Till soil in fall and spring to destroy overwintering pupae
Encourage braconid wasps (do not disturb hornworms with white cocoons)
Use black light at night to find fluorescent hornworms
Interplant with basil and marigolds to deter moths
Rotate crops annually
remedies: Hand-pick hornworms and relocate or destroy them
Leave hornworms covered with white parasitic wasp cocoons (they'll die naturally)
Apply Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) spray to foliage
Use spinosad-based insecticide for young caterpillars
Release Trichogramma wasps to parasitize eggs
Till soil after harvest to expose and kill pupae
Spray with neem oil to deter feeding

pests.name: Colorado Potato Beetle
pests.description: Distinctive yellow-orange beetles with black stripes that devastate potato and nightshade family crops. Both adults and larvae feed voraciously on foliage.
symptoms: Rapid defoliation of potato and nightshade plants
Yellow-orange beetles with 10 black stripes on wing covers
Orange-red egg masses on leaf undersides
Red-orange humpbacked larvae feeding on leaves
Skeletonized leaves and completely stripped plants
Reduced tuber/fruit yield and size
precautions: Practice crop rotation — move potato family at least 200 feet yearly
Use thick mulch (straw) to impede beetle emergence
Plant resistant potato varieties when available
Use floating row covers until flowering
Till soil in fall to expose overwintering adults
Interplant with tansy, catnip, or horseradish
remedies: Hand-pick adults, larvae, and crush egg masses
Apply Bacillus thuringiensis var. tenebrionis (Btt) for larvae
Use spinosad spray — effective against larvae and adults
Apply neem oil to repel adults and disrupt larval development
Use kaolin clay (Surround WP) as a physical barrier
Apply diatomaceous earth around plants
For severe infestations, rotate chemical classes to prevent resistance

pests.name: Flea Beetles
pests.description: Tiny, jumping beetles that chew small round holes in leaves. They are particularly damaging to seedlings and young transplants in spring.
symptoms: Numerous small round 'shot holes' in leaves
Leaves appearing lace-like with many tiny perforations
Wilting and death of young seedlings
Tiny, shiny black or bronze beetles that jump when disturbed
Scarred or pitted stems near soil line
Delayed plant growth and reduced vigor
precautions: Use floating row covers on seedlings until plants are established
Delay transplanting until plants are larger and more tolerant
Use white or reflective mulch to disorient beetles
Remove crop debris promptly after harvest
Start seedlings indoors for larger transplants
Interplant with catnip or basil as repellent companions
remedies: Apply diatomaceous earth to foliage (reapply after rain)
Use kaolin clay spray to create a physical barrier on leaves
Apply neem oil spray every 5-7 days
Use spinosad-based insecticide for heavy infestations
Apply pyrethrin spray for immediate knockdown
Set up yellow sticky traps near affected plants
Use beneficial nematodes to control soil-dwelling larvae

pests.name: Sawflies
pests.description: Larvae of wasp-like insects that resemble caterpillars. They feed gregariously on leaves and can rapidly defoliate roses, willows, and other plants.
symptoms: Leaves skeletonized or eaten from edges inward
Groups of slug-like larvae feeding together on leaves
Window-paning (larvae eat one leaf surface, leaving the other)
Rolled or curled leaves containing larvae
Rapid defoliation of affected branches
Slug-like larvae (smooth, green or brown) often with dark heads
precautions: Inspect plants regularly in spring and early summer
Cultivate soil around plants in fall to expose pupae
Encourage bird populations in the garden
Remove and destroy egg-laying sites on leaf edges
Maintain plant vigor through proper care
Use horticultural fleece on susceptible plants
remedies: Hand-pick larvae and drop in soapy water
Spray with insecticidal soap directly on larvae
Apply neem oil spray to foliage
Use pyrethrin-based spray for heavy infestations
Note: Bt (Bacillus thuringiensis) is NOT effective against sawflies
Apply spinosad spray for effective control
Cultivate soil around plants to expose and kill pupae

pests.name: Stink Bugs
pests.description: Shield-shaped bugs that pierce plant tissue and suck out fluids. The brown marmorated stink bug is an invasive species causing increasing agricultural damage.
symptoms: Dimpled, distorted, or cat-faced fruit
Cloudy or discolored spots on tomatoes and peppers
Brown or white feeding scars on fruit surfaces
Seeds damaged inside pods (soybeans, beans)
Strong unpleasant odor when bugs are crushed
Shield-shaped brown or green bugs on plants
precautions: Seal cracks around home foundations to prevent indoor invasions
Remove weeds and overgrown vegetation near garden edges
Use trap crops like sunflowers or sweet corn to lure bugs away
Install row covers over susceptible crops
Eliminate overwintering sites (brush piles, leaf litter)
Monitor with pheromone traps starting in spring
remedies: Hand-pick bugs and drop into soapy water
Use kaolin clay spray as a deterrent on fruit crops
Apply neem oil to reduce feeding damage
Use pyrethrin spray for quick knockdown
Set up light traps (stink bugs are attracted to light at night)
Release Trissolcus japonicus parasitic wasp (where approved)
Apply bifenthrin to garden borders (perimeter treatment)
